errors.excludedReason: You can raise the nRMSE threshold in advanced options, or try other parameters (mass, Crr, position).

crrPreset.modernRoadTubeless: Modern road tubeless (GP5000 TLR, Corsa Pro) — default
crrPreset.raceTubeless: Race tubeless (GP5000 S TR, Pro One TLR)

dashboard.meanCda: Mean CdA ({{count}} ride kept out of {{total}}) - inverse-variance method
dashboard.meanCda_plural: Mean CdA ({{count}} rides kept out of {{total}}) - inverse-variance method
dashboard.exclusionFooter: Exclusion: nRMSE > 45%, CdA outside [{{min}}-{{max}}] m², or degenerate solver (bound hit / non-identifiable). Hover an excluded ride for the exact reason.

position.more_aero_hint: A more aero position like {{label}} (ref. {{value}}) could save you up to {{gain}} m² - if your body follows.

refTable.cdaLead: Where does your CdA sit compared to reference positions?
refTable.crrLead: Where does your Crr sit compared to reference tyres/surfaces?

r2.label: Road, aero drops
r2.desc: Drops, flat back, good aero reflexes

r4.label: Road, hands on tops
r4.desc: Torso upright, hands on the bar top

r5.label: Upright / MTB
r5.desc: MTB, city bike, very upright position

r1.label: Tubeless clincher, tarmac
r1.desc: GP5000 TL, Corsa, Pro One, etc.

r2.label: Standard clincher
r2.desc: Classic road tyre with inner tube

profile.title: Your profile
profile.desc: Rider weight, bike type and riding position. Usually already filled by your active profile.

tires.title: Your tyres
tires.desc: Fixing Crr improves CdA precision. Pick the preset closest to your setup.

ride.title: Your ride
ride.desc: Drop one or several .FIT / .GPX / .TCX files. Multiple rides = more reliable result.

rides.title: Your Intervals rides
rides.desc: Pick the date window then list your rides. Fine-grained filters live in advanced options.

connection.title: Intervals.icu connection
connection.desc: Your API key stays in this browser — nothing is sent to our servers beyond the proxied calls to intervals.icu.

advanced.title: Advanced options
advanced.desc: Drivetrain efficiency, wind factor, quality threshold. Leave at defaults if unsure.

fileUpload.dropHint: Multiple rides = more accurate averaged result (bad ones are filtered out automatically)
fileUpload.crrFixHint: Fixing Crr gives a more stable CdA. Select your tyres if you know them.
fileUpload.positionUnsure: Not sure about your position? Pick "I don't know" in the selector.
fileUpload.manualWindHint: Fill these two fields if the verdict shows 'fragile wind' or if you have a reliable measurement (station, Windy, Tempest). Leave empty to keep Open-Meteo.

intervals.title: Intervals.icu
intervals.keepUnfiltered: Keep all rides, don't filter on solver agreement
intervals.errAnalysis: Analysis error - corrupted file or pre-processing failed
intervals.errSolver: Solver disagreement, bound hit, or poorly-conditioned Hessian
intervals.errBounds: CdA estimated outside the physical bounds for this bike type
intervals.flatSpeedTooltip: Average power / CdA = how fast you can go on the flat.
intervals.apiKeyTooltip: Find your API key in Intervals.icu → Settings → Developer Settings (bottom of the page).
intervals.athleteIdTooltip: Visible in the URL of your Intervals.icu profile (e.g. i12345). Or set '0' for the logged-in user.

qualityQual.permissive: permissive — more rides but less accurate

intervals.solverConfTooltipOff: Keep all rides, don't filter on solver agreement
intervals.solverConfTooltipMedium: Exclude rides where |ΔCdA wind-chung| ≥ 0.05 (strong disagreement) AND rides where a solver hit a physical bound (cross-check unreliable)
intervals.solverConfTooltipHigh: Keep only rides where |ΔCdA wind-chung| < 0.02 (solvers agree) and no solver at the bound
intervals.inverseVarTooltip: Weighted mean by number of valid points × quality (1/nRMSE). Rides with nRMSE > 45% or CdA out of bounds are excluded.
intervals.hksjTooltip: HKSJ (Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman): small-k correction applied because n<10. The CI95 is widened with a t-quantile instead of 1.96 and a factor q that reinjects the empirical dispersion. Ref. IntHout et al., BMC Med Res Methodol 2014.
intervals.nEffTooltip: N = number of rides in the meta-analysis. n_eff = effective sample size after random-effects weighting (Σwᵢ)²/Σwᵢ². If n_eff << N, the estimate is dominated by a few rides (small σ_i).
intervals.reasonModelImperfect: imperfect physics model (API wind or unusual position)
intervals.priorDominated: result dominated by the prior — data not very informative
intervals.errNrmse: nRMSE too high: the physical model doesn't reproduce the measured power
intervals.chungFallback: Each ride is also analysed with Chung VE as a cross-check.
intervals.crossCheckHint: Prioritise the 'without prior' values to detect cases where the two solvers converge to the bound (artificial agreement). Filter off by default.
intervals.outdoorOnly: Only outdoor rides with a power meter are taken into account (indoor and rides without power are always excluded).
intervals.groupHint: (keyword detection: group, peloton, with, draft, race…)
intervals.gradeHint: Excludes steep climbs: at steep grade, aero drag is drowned by gravity and CdA becomes non-identifiable. 5 m/km (0.5%) = velodrome/flat road; 10 m/km (1%) = rolling flat; 15 m/km (1.5%) = gentle hills.
intervals.excludedByGrade_one: ⓘ {{count}} ride excluded by 'max mean grade' ({{val}} m/km) — CdA not identifiable on high elevation.
intervals.excludedByGrade_other: ⓘ {{count}} rides excluded by 'max mean grade' ({{val}} m/km) — CdA not identifiable on high elevation.

history.title: Progress
history.emptyDesc: Run an analysis to see it appear here.
history.disclaimer: Your analyses are stored only in your browser (localStorage), never on a server. They can be lost if you clear the cache or change device - export regularly to keep a backup.
history.exportTitle: Download all analyses in JSON format
history.importTitle: Import a JSON history (merges with existing)
history.stabilityTitle: CdA stability (rolling std. dev. over {{n}} rides)
history.stabilityDesc: Standard deviation (σ) of your CdA over the last {{n}} rides at each date. Lower is better - your estimates are reproducible. A sharp drop = sensor change or better calibration. Grey zones indicate windows below the good-quality threshold.
history.biasDistDesc: Bias = measured power / theoretical power (CdA prior, Crr=0.005) on flat sections. A well-calibrated sensor peaks at 1.0 with a tight distribution. A drifting sensor shows a wide or off-centre distribution.
history.missingBiasTooltip: {{count}} rides without recorded bias (old entries or failed regression).
history.outOfRangeTooltip: {{count}} rides outside [0.6, 1.6] not shown (very miscalibrated sensors or sensor bug).
history.noStabilityData: Not enough data for stability chart.
history.noBiasData: Not enough data for bias distribution.

compare.title: Multi-rider comparison
compare.subtitle: Drag-drop multiple files per rider for more accurate averaged results. Formats: .FIT, .GPX, .TCX.
compare.draftingBody: {{drafter}} has a CdA {{pct}}% lower than {{puller}} at similar speed. {{drafter}} probably followed the wheels.
compare.bestAeroTooltip: Lowest CdA (weighted average over all rides).
compare.bestWCdaTooltip: W/CdA = average power / CdA. The fastest rouleur on the flat: combines fitness (watts) and aero (low CdA). The flat-terrain analogue of W/kg.
compare.mostEfficientTooltip: Lowest total drag force (aero + rolling) at 40 km/h.
compare.priorHeavilyReinforcedTitle: prior very strongly reinforced ×{{factor}} — data not very informative

diag.windModerate: Wind may be imprecise (CdA moderately sensitive)
diag.windBodyFragile: Strong API wind (≥ 4 m/s) on a highly sensitive ride (±30% of wind moves CdA by more than 0.05 m²). This is the scenario where ERA5 / Open-Meteo can be imprecise (coast, relief, strong wind). Enter measured wind for a reliable analysis.
diag.windBodyModerate: The CdA shifts by 0.025 to 0.05 m² when the API wind is perturbed by ±30%. Moderate sensitivity - a measured wind would improve precision, but the estimate is still usable.
diag.windBodyRobust: Perturbing the API wind by ±30% leaves the CdA almost unchanged. Robust estimate.
diag.windApiSummary: API wind used: {{ms}} m/s ({{kmh}} km/h) from {{dir}}°
diag.manualWindHelp: Enter the real wind at rider level (not at 10 m). Examples: Météo-France stations, Windy, Weatherflow Tempest, or an estimate based on feel on the bike.
diag.unreliableTitle: CdA and Crr not estimable on this ride (R² = {{r2}})
diag.unreliableBody1: The physical model does not apply: one or more forces are not accounted for (real wind very different from API weather, heavy drafting, dragging brakes, faulty sensor…). The values below are not reliable - the solver found the 'least bad' fit possible, not a correct measurement.
diag.unreliableBody2: What you can still use: the flat / climb / descent breakdown below (still qualitatively informative), and the direction of the residuals bias in the alerts (indicates whether the sensor is over- or under-calibrated).
diag.badFitBody: The physical model does not explain this ride well. Likely causes: miscalibrated power meter, very noisy GPS altitude, lots of drafting or braking, or a ride poorly suited to the method (e-bike, cyclocross, MTB in trees). Treat the CdA and Crr values with strong caution.
diag.pmBiasHigh: **Sensor probably miscalibrated.** On flat pedalled sections ({{n}} points), the measured power is {{pct}}% higher than the theoretical value for a typical CdA/Crr. This is the sign of a sensor with a stuck offset or a missing zero-offset. Recalibrate before the next ride.
diag.pmBiasMild: Measured power {{pct}}% above the theoretical value ({{n}} flat points). May indicate a slight calibration drift, or simply a stronger-than-average rider profile.
diag.pmBiasLow: Measured power {{pct}}% below the theoretical value ({{n}} flat points). Sensor under-reading or strong unmodelled tailwind.
diag.windSensTooltip: Post-hoc: Chung VE re-run with wind_speed × 1.05. The exposed Δ shows whether CdA is robust (|Δ|<0.005, green), moderate (<0.015, amber) or fragile (red) to the Open-Meteo wind bias. ERA5 has a documented ~-0.7% mean bias and under-estimates high winds (Jourdier 2020). Replaces a blind correction with a transparent diagnostic.
diag.windFragilityTooltip: Sensitivity test: Chung VE re-run with wind_speed × 1.30 and × 0.70 (plausible bounds of the ERA5 bias in coastal zones, Jourdier 2020). wind_fragility = max(|Δ±30%|) classified robust < 0.02 < moderate < 0.05 ≤ fragile.

breakdown.title: CdA by gradient regime
breakdown.subtitle: Big asymmetry = sign of bias OR real position change
breakdown.asymmetryWarn: {{spread}} m² spread between regimes - check that the API wind is representative of the ride.

derived.title: Power to ride on the flat
derived.aeroAt40: At 40 km/h: {{share}}% of the power overcomes the air, the rest is rolling resistance.

charts.altitudeDesc: Compares the real GPS altitude (green) to the 'virtual' altitude rebuilt from the energy balance with the estimated CdA/Crr. If the estimates are correct, the two curves stay close. A growing gap indicates a systematic bias (sensor, weight, or wind estimated wrong).
charts.cdaRollingDesc: Instantaneous CdA on a rolling 10-minute window. Should stay roughly flat around the horizontal line (mean CdA). Peaks or dips = position change, drafting, or zones where the model is unreliable.
charts.powerDecompDesc: Breaks down total power into components: aero (apparent wind force), rolling (tyre/ground friction) and gravity (climbs). Lets you see which force dominates at each moment and where an aero improvement would matter most.
charts.powerScatterDesc: Each point compares predicted power (Y axis) to measured power (X axis). Good fit = points aligned on the red diagonal. Scattered = imprecise model on this ride.
charts.residualsDesc: Gap between model and measured power, in watts. Should be centred on 0 and bell-shaped. A systematic offset (mean ≠ 0) suggests bias: miscalibrated sensor, wrong weight, or under/over-estimated wind.
charts.speedPowerDesc: Relationship between ground speed and measured power. Scatter comes from gradient, wind, and acceleration. Compact cloud = uniform conditions. Very scattered = varied ride (mountain, changing wind).
charts.energyPieDesc: Share of total energy spent against air (aero), friction (rolling), gravity (climbs), and acceleration. On the flat above 30 km/h, aero dominates (~70-80%). In the mountains, gravity takes over.
charts.speedCdaDesc: Relationship between instantaneous CdA and measured power. A strong correlation suggests a bias (sensor or Crr estimated wrong) that depends on the power regime.
charts.windChartDesc: Wind speed (blue, km/h) and bearing (red dotted, degrees; 0°=N, 90°=E, 180°=S, 270°=W) along the route. Shows whether wind stayed constant or changed, which directly impacts model quality.
charts.rhoDesc: Air density variation along the route. ρ drops with altitude and rises with cold. Same CdA produces less drag at low ρ (altitude). On a 0-to-1500 m ride, ρ can vary by 15% — that's why it's computed per point.
charts.mapDesc: GPS trace on a basemap. Coloured by rolling CdA (see legend below the map) for a quick read of where the position held or drifted.

landing.heroLede: No velodrome. No protocol. No monthly fee. Drop a <fit>.fit</fit>, get a CdA with a calibrated confidence interval - and the complete methodology behind every number.
landing.byline: Built by Loïc Bouxirot · AI engineer at Imperial College
landing.diffHeadline: Six choices that make this tool trustworthy.

windInverse.title: Wind-inverse solver
windInverse.body: The primary solver jointly fits CdA and the wind field, rather than trusting a 10 km-grid weather API. On rides with heading variety this typically doubles R² (0.5 → 0.98).

hierarchical.title: Hierarchical meta-analysis
hierarchical.body: Multi-ride aggregation uses a DerSimonian-Laird random-effects estimator with the Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman small-sample correction. Proper meta-analysis, not a naïve average.

priorInvariance.title: Prior invariance, tested
priorInvariance.body: Your position prior should not shift the aggregate CdA. AeroProfile publishes cda_raw on every ride so you can verify, and a pytest regression enforces the invariant in CI.

qualityGates.title: Eight quality gates
qualityGates.body: Not every ride yields a good CdA. Each analysis is classified by 8 documented gates (solvers_pegged, sensor_miscalib, prior_dominated…) so bad rides are flagged, not silently averaged in.

windSens.title: Per-ride wind sensitivity
windSens.body: A post-hoc re-solve with wind × 1.05 exposes the Δ CdA. Not a blind correction - a transparent number showing whether this ride is wind-robust or wind-fragile.

pmDrift.title: Power-meter drift detection
pmDrift.body: Independent of the solver. Flat-pedaling bias ratio tracked per sensor, in your history, with a KDE distribution. Spots a drifting Assioma before your CdA starts to look weird.

landing.howItWorksHeadline: From a bike file to a trustworthy CdA in ~30 seconds.
landing.step1Body: .fit, .gpx or .tcx. One file or a batch - batches get aggregated with proper hierarchical meta-analysis, not a naïve mean.
landing.step2Body: 13 filters isolate steady-state points. Three solvers compete (wind-inverse, Chung VE, Martin LS). Eight quality gates grade the estimate. Tiled Open-Meteo weather feeds the wind.
landing.step3Title: You get a number you can trust
landing.step3Body: CdA with Hessian + conformal confidence intervals. Per-regime breakdown (flat / climb / descent). Diagnostics if something smells off. And every choice explained in /methods.
landing.methodsLede: Every threshold, prior, and solver choice in AeroProfile is documented. Here are three favourites.

featuredBlurbs.windCorrection: Why AeroProfile doesn't trust Open-Meteo's wind at face value - and how a Bayesian inverse problem over the wind field doubles R² on rides with heading variety.
featuredBlurbs.priorInvariance: If your position prior shifts the aggregate CdA, the tool is broken. How AeroProfile guarantees invariance in CI, with a real 4iiii dataset case study.
featuredBlurbs.aggregation: DerSimonian-Laird random-effects estimator with HKSJ small-sample correction. When to use it, and why 'average all the rides' isn't good enough.

landing.builderPitch: Aero is half of your speed. Nobody measures it.

lapSelector.title: Laps
lapSelector.tooShortHint: Lap too short to be analysed (< 30 s)

about.title: How a cycling question spiralled into a Bayesian solver.
about.p1: <name>Loïc</name>. Cyclist, with the unfortunate habit of not letting a question go unanswered — which, eventually, leads to projects like this one.
about.p2: My dad and I have been riding together for a while. Same corner of Brittany, same wind, same coffee at the end, and every time the same gap: ten-odd watts between us. He's a bit heavier than I am, sure, but we ride in Brittany — not the Alps — so weight doesn't explain much. The most plausible suspect is CdA, a single number that captures how you push against the air: your position, the shape of your helmet, how you hold the bars. Dad and I probably don't slice the air the same way. Verifying that normally requires a velodrome, a fairly demanding protocol, or a €600 onboard sensor — which, for a question only two Breton cyclists are asking, feels mildly disproportionate.
about.p3: Hence the idea: what if a single .fit file were enough? You upload your ride — .gpx and .tcx work too — and the tool returns a CdA estimate with a calibrated confidence interval, no velodrome and no perfect conditions required. The actual hard part is that wind and drag don't separate cleanly: pin one to estimate the other and you're going to be wrong. That's where a solver that fits wind and drag jointly earns its keep — not for show, but because it's the only honest way to disentangle the two, leaning on weather data when the ride alone leaves several plausible answers on the table. A hierarchical meta-analysis then combines the results across multiple rides without flattening the uncertainty, and automatic guardrails drop files too noisy to be exploitable. Fourteen methodology articles document every choice, for those who really want to go down the rabbit hole — or who don't trust me, which is entirely fair.
about.p4: The whole thing is open source, on GitHub. Bug reports are welcome, even better with a .fit file attached. Pushback on the physics or the statistics is also welcome: come with your arguments and your file.
about.ctaAnalyzeBody: Upload your .fit, .gpx or .tcx file and get a CdA estimate in seconds.
about.ctaMethodsBody: 14 articles document the physical and statistical choices behind the tool.

footer.tagline: Open-source CdA estimator for cyclists. Built by Loïc Bouxirot. AGPL-3.0 licensed. Your ride data stays in your browser - nothing is stored on any server.

facts.1: Your CdA matters as much as your fitness: an amateur tucked in (CdA 0.28) beats a pro upright (CdA 0.42) at equal power.
facts.2: Doubling your power does not double your speed: aero drag scales with speed cubed. +50% watts = only +14% km/h.
facts.3: At 35 km/h on the flat, 80% of your power fights the air. The rest: tyres, transmission, accelerations.
facts.4: A skinsuit saves more watts than aero wheels. Spend your budget in the right order.
facts.5: The Chung method estimates your CdA without a wind tunnel. It compares your GPS elevation to the one reconstructed from the energy balance.
facts.6: Open-Meteo gives wind at 10 m. At rider level (1.3 m), it's roughly 65% of that in open terrain.
facts.7: A small rider (5ft7, 145 lbs) will naturally have a CdA around 0.26. A taller one (6ft3, 190 lbs) closer to 0.34.
facts.8: TT extensions + flat back can save 30 W at 40 km/h. That's two years of training.

s1.title: In one sentence
s1.body: AeroProfile is a client-side tool: your activity files (FIT / GPX / TCX) are analysed in your browser, the server does not retain them, and no advertising profile is built on you. The rest of this page details exactly what travels, where, and why.

s2.title: What we process, and where
s2.body: When you upload an activity file, three things happen:
s2.item1: The file is sent to our server for parsing and analysis (Chung VE, wind_inverse, etc.). It stays in memory for the duration of the computation (a few seconds), then is discarded without being written to disk.
s2.item2: The result (CdA, Crr, charts) is sent back to your browser and cached locally in your localStorage so we don't recompute if you drop the same file again.
s2.item3: Nothing is linked to an account, email, or identity - there are no user accounts on AeroProfile.

s3.title: What is stored in your browser
s3.body: Everything stays on your device via localStorage:
s3.item1: Your analysis history (CdA, Crr, ride metadata) for the History module.
s3.item2: Your athlete profiles (mass, bike type, position) to pre-fill the form.
s3.item3: Your Intervals.icu API token (if you use the integration) - never forwarded to our server beyond the proxied calls to intervals.icu.

s4.title: Third parties
s4.body: To do its job, AeroProfile calls two external services: <openmeteo></openmeteo> to fetch weather data (wind, temperature, pressure) matching your ride's date and GPS coordinates, and, only if you explicitly opt in, <intervals></intervals> to import your activities (token provided by you, stored only in your browser).

s5.title: Analytics
s5.body: AeroProfile uses a minimal analytics stack (Vercel Analytics) that counts page views and usage events (Analyze click, export). No third-party cookies, no fingerprinting, no sharing with ad networks. You can block this tracking via your usual blocker without breaking the tool.

s6.title: Your rights
s6.body: Under GDPR, you have the right:
s6.item1: Of access: all your data lives in your browser's localStorage (DevTools → Application → Local Storage).
s6.item2: To erasure: clear the localStorage (or use the Reset button in Settings).
s6.item3: To portability: export your history as JSON from the History panel.
s6.item4: To opt out: you can use AeroProfile without creating an account (there is none) and nothing is retained server-side after the computation.

s7.title: Changes to this policy
s7.body: Significant changes will be mentioned here with the update date. AeroProfile is open source - you can follow the full history on GitHub.

blogIndexHeader.title: How AeroProfile calculates your CdA - every step, every assumption.
blogIndexHeader.lede: 14 articles covering the physics, the algorithms, and the statistical choices inside the tool. Formulas included, explained in plain language.

roadDrops.name: Road, drops
roadDrops.description: Hands in the drops, moderately flat back

roadTops.name: Road, upright
roadTops.description: Hands on the tops, torso upright

upright.name: Upright / MTB
upright.description: Upright position, MTB or city-bike style

tooltips.derivedAeroShare: Fraction of power spent against aerodynamic drag (the rest goes into rolling resistance). At low speed the wheel dominates; at high speed the air dominates (v² law for force, v³ for power).
tooltips.gradientBreakdown: CdA recomputed separately on the climbing (>+2%), descending (<-2%) and flat (±2%) portions. A > 0.08 m² gap between the three suggests a bias: asymmetric wind poorly captured, sensor drift at high power, or position change between climbing and descending. It's not necessarily an error — riders DO sit up on slow climbs.
tooltips.heroCda: CdA = drag coefficient × frontal area (m²). Hessian CI = 95% confidence interval based on the likelihood curvature at the optimum. Conformal CI = distribution-free interval with a formal 95% coverage guarantee on the rider's history.
tooltips.solverDeltaMedian: Median of (Chung VE CdA − wind_inverse CdA) on your past clean rides. Measures the systematic disagreement between the two solvers on YOUR dataset, which isn't captured by the Hessian CI.
tooltips.priorOffExplain: Estimate obtained by disabling the CdA prior. The wind and Crr priors stay active to keep the problem well-posed. A gap > 0.05 with the main value triggers the 'prior_dominated' status.
tooltips.crrSecondary: Crr = rolling-resistance coefficient (dimensionless). Depends on tyres, pressure, and surface. Typically 0.003-0.004 on a well-inflated road tyre on smooth tarmac, 0.005-0.007 on rough road, 0.007-0.010 on gravel.
tooltips.rmseSecondary: RMSE (root-mean-square error) between modelled and measured power. On a real ride, 15-25 W is typical; under 15 W is excellent; above 30 W there's a bias. The complementary R² depends on the ride's variance.
tooltips.rhoSecondary: Mean air density over the ride. Varies with altitude, temperature, and humidity. ~1.22 at sea level at 15 °C, ~1.05 at 1500 m.
tooltips.windSecondary: Mean wind over the ride, fetched from Open-Meteo. Direction is the bearing the wind comes from (0°=N, 90°=E, 180°=S, 270°=W). Wind is corrected to rider height (factor 0.7).
tooltips.runningAvg: Dots = per-ride CdA. Line = quality-weighted cumulative mean. The more the line stabilises, the more trustworthy the mean CdA. The first rides move the average; after 5-6 rides it converges.
tooltips.evolChart: Each point is one analysed ride. If CdA decreases over time, the rider improved their aero position (or changed equipment). Caveat: CdA also varies with conditions (wind, drafting, route). Points with R² < 0.3 are more uncertain.
tooltips.whatIf: Vary a parameter and see the impact on the ride. The model recomputes point by point with the Martin equation, keeping the route and weather identical.
tooltips.positionRef: The reference is the Bayesian prior used by the solver — not a ground truth. It represents the mean expected value for this position on this bike type, from the literature (Debraux et al. 2011) and wind-tunnel measurements.
tooltips.positionInput: Your reference position acts as a prior (starting hypothesis) for the solver. It doesn't fix your CdA — it just tells the model a likely value to search around, to stabilise the estimate on noisy rides. The more aero the position (e.g. aero bars), the lower the expected prior. If unsure, pick the closest match to your actual posture on the bike — the solver will adjust.
tooltips.icBroad: 95% confidence interval that combines three sources of uncertainty: the likelihood curvature (Hessian), wind sensitivity (max |ΔCdA| when API wind is perturbed ±30%), and a (1 − R²) penalty term that widens the interval on poorly-fitted rides. More honest than the Hessian-only CI, which only captures statistical fit precision and ignores model bias.
tooltips.icHessianLimit: Hessian CI = pure curvature of the likelihood at the optimum. It says: 'if I nudge CdA a little, the fit gets worse at this rate'. But it does NOT include wind uncertainty or fit quality (R²). On a ride with R² < 0.5, this CI can look very tight even though the result is actually unreliable — prefer the broadened CI above.
tooltips.cdaRefRanges: Typical CdA ranges from Debraux et al. 2011 and wind-tunnel measurements. Your value is highlighted in the matching range. CdA depends on the position on the bike, the rider's morphology, and equipment (helmet, kit).
tooltips.crrRefRanges: Typical rolling-resistance coefficient ranges. Depends on the tyre (model, pressure, width), the surface, and the temperature. Low Crr = a tyre that rolls well on smooth surfaces.

t0.name: Formula 1
t0.desc: You slice through the air like a GP single-seater. Elite TT position.

t1.name: Gliding emperor penguin
t1.desc: As streamlined as a penguin on ice. Pro-level TT position.

t2.name: Diving eagle
t2.desc: Wings tucked, you dive through the air like a raptor. Solid amateur TT.

t3.name: Dolphin
t3.desc: Perfect hydrodynamics. Aggressive road position, hands in the drops, flat back.

t4.name: Football
t4.desc: Surprisingly aero thanks to the seams. Road drops, good compromise.

t5.name: Downhill skier
t5.desc: Tuck-and-go position. Road hoods, nothing to be ashamed of.

t6.name: Galloping lion
t6.desc: Powerful but not very streamlined. Standard hoods, room to improve on the torso.

t7.name: Brick on its side
t7.desc: Not the most aero at the zoo. Upright, hands on the tops.

t8.name: Bear on its hind legs
t8.desc: Imposing into the wind. Very upright, MTB or city bike.

t9.name: Barn door
t9.desc: You offer your whole surface to the wind. Work to do! 😄

chartsLegend.filteredHint: — grey zones = segments excluded by iterative refinement

evolChart.title: CdA evolution over time
evolChart.subtitle: One point per ride — shows whether the aero position has improved.

runningAvgChart.title: Mean CdA convergence
runningAvgChart.subtitle: The running average stabilises over the rides

categoryDesc.Fundamentals: The physics and vocabulary every reader should start with.
categoryDesc.Algorithms: Solvers, filters, and the pipeline architecture.
categoryDesc.Statistics: Priors, meta-analysis, and the regression tests that guard them.
categoryDesc.Diagnostics: What to inspect when a number looks wrong.

power-equation.title: The cycling power equation: Martin et al. (1998)
power-equation.desc: How 4 physical forces (aero, rolling, gravity, acceleration) determine the power you have to produce. The foundation of everything.

cda-what-is-it.title: CdA: what is it and why does it matter?
cda-what-is-it.desc: The aerodynamic drag coefficient explained simply. How it varies with your position, your equipment, and why 0.01 of CdA makes a real difference.

choose-position.title: Which position should I pick in the form?
choose-position.desc: The Position selector is not a trap: it doesn't fix your CdA, it just helps the solver get started. How to fill this field, and what to pick if you really aren't sure.

wind-correction.title: Wind correction: from the weather API to wind-inverse
wind-correction.desc: Wind is the biggest source of error. How we correct it: Open-Meteo API, spatial tiling, 10 m → 1 m log profile, and inverse estimation from your data.

solvers.title: The 3 AeroProfile solvers: Martin LS, Chung VE, Wind-Inverse
solvers.desc: Why a single solver isn't enough, and how AeroProfile automatically picks the best method for each ride.

filters.title: Data filtering: how we separate signal from noise
filters.desc: 13 filters, from braking to drafting, and why every excluded point makes the result more reliable.

iterative-refinement.title: Iterative refinement: only use the points where the model works
iterative-refinement.desc: Two automatic passes: solve, flag zones where real / virtual altitude diverge, exclude, re-solve. Like Golden Cheetah, but automated.

drafting-detection.title: Drafting detection: when riding in a group fools the CdA
drafting-detection.desc: Why apparent CdA drops 30-40% in a peloton, how we detect it, and what we can still learn.

yaw-angle.title: Yaw angle: why crosswinds change your CdA
yaw-angle.desc: When the wind hits sideways, your drag rises. How we correct to report a 'wind-tunnel' CdA.

bayesian-priors.title: Bayesian priors: how to stabilise the solver
bayesian-priors.desc: When data is insufficient, a soft prior stops the solver from diverging. Intuitive and mathematical explanation. Includes the multi-ride prior trap.

aggregation-methods.title: Multi-ride aggregation: inverse-variance vs hierarchical
aggregation-methods.desc: How AeroProfile combines N rides into a single representative CdA: precision-weighted mean (method A) and DerSimonian-Laird hierarchical meta-analysis.

prior-invariance.title: Prior invariance: why your position choice should not move the aggregated CdA
prior-invariance.desc: The most powerful consistency test in meta-analysis: rerun the same analysis with two different priors. When invariance breaks it's almost always a convergence bug - and the story of a fix on a noisy 4iiii single-leg dataset.

power-meter-quality.title: The power meter: the error source the solver cannot correct
power-meter-quality.desc: Why single-leg sensors (4iiii, Stages left) give a 2× more variable CdA, and how AeroProfile detects a calibration bias independently of the solver.

w-cda-metric.title: W/CdA: the rouleur's metric (the W/kg for flat terrain)
w-cda-metric.desc: Power over aero drag determines your flat-terrain speed. W/CdA → km/h lookup table included.

intervals-integration.title: Intervals.icu integration: analyse a year of rides in one click
intervals-integration.desc: Connect your account, filter your rides, and get a weighted-average CdA over dozens of rides. The 4 filtering levels explained.

profile.label: Profile:
profile.newTooltip: Create a new profile with the current form settings
profile.helpUpload: Your choices (mass, bike, position, Crr, thresholds) are saved automatically into the active profile. To manage multiple setups, create a new profile from the current form.
profile.helpIntervals: Your choices (Intervals credentials, mass, bike, position, filters, dates, sensors) are saved automatically into the active profile. To manage multiple setups, create a new profile from the current state.
